topics.title: Implementation and validation of displacement controller with microcontrollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL508 - PCCPL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation and validation of displacement controller with microcontrollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **displacement controller** is a closed-loop feedback control system in which the process variable (PV) being regulated is a linear or angular positional quantity (e.g., translation in millimetres or rotation in degrees). The objective of the controller is to force the measured displacement to track a user-defined **setpoint (SP)** with minimum **steady-state error**, acceptable **settling time**, negligible **overshoot**, and robust **disturbance rejection**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Process Control & Process Instrumentation laboratory, course code PCCPL508), the experiment typically involves implementing the control law in firmware on a **microcontroller development board** (Arduino Uno / Mega, MSP430, STM32 Nucleo, or PIC) and validating its performance against a **physical actuator–sensor rig** (e.g., a DC-geared motor driving a leadscrew with an LVDT or rotary encoder as the position transducer).

Mathematically, the canonical **discrete-time PID control law** implemented in the microcontroller is:

$$u(k) = K_p \, e(k) + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)\,T_s + K_d \,\frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

where $u(k)$ is the control effort at sample instant $k$, $e(k) = SP - y(k)$ is the positional error, $T_s$ is the sampling period, and $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$ are the **proportional, integral, and derivative gains** respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment tests the learner's ability to (a) derive the discrete equivalent of a continuous PID, (b) code it efficiently, (c) implement **anti-windup** and **derivative filtering**, and (d) experimentally validate transient and steady-state performance metrics as per Outcome-Based Education (OBE) frameworks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car into a narrow parking spot** marked by a wall.

- Your **eyes** measure the remaining displacement to the wall → this is the *sensor* (e.g., LVDT, encoder).
- Your **brain** computes how much to turn the steering wheel and how hard to press the accelerator → this is the *control algorithm* running on the microcontroller.
- Your **hands and feet** actuate the car → these are the *motor driver and DC motor* (or servo/stepper).

If you are far from the spot, you press the accelerator hard (large proportional action). As you approach, you ease off (proportional reduces). Your accumulated past mistakes correct for slope/gradient (integral action). If you see the wall approaching too fast, you brake (derivative action). The microcontroller does exactly this, but in **discrete numerical steps** at fixed intervals (e.g., every **10 ms**, so $T_s = 0.01$ s).

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Constants

The following standard metrics are universally used by KTU evaluators when grading displacement controller experiments:

- **Sampling period ($T_s$):** typically **1 ms – 100 ms** (Nyquist criterion: $T_s \leq T_{settling}/10$).
- **ADC resolution:** **10-bit (1024 levels)** on Arduino Uno → quantum $= 5\text{ V} / 1024 \approx 4.88$ mV.
- **PWM resolution:** **8-bit (0–255)** duty cycle on Arduino `analogWrite()`.
- **Sensor non-linearity budget:** LVDT $\leq \pm 0.5\%$, encoder $\pm 1$ LSB (Least Significant Bit).
- **Standard performance indices:** **ISE, IAE, ITAE, MSE, RMSE**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Integral Time Absolute Error (ITAE)** criterion is heavily favoured in KTU valuation because it penalises long-duration errors more than initial ones — making it the gold standard for displacement (servo) tuning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Microcontroller-Based Displacement Loop

The closed loop can be decomposed into five functional blocks, each with a precise role:

1. **Setpoint Generator:** Produces the desired displacement reference $r(t)$ (e.g., a step input, ramp, or multi-level sequence).
2. **Sensor Stage:** Converts physical displacement $y(t)$ into a proportional voltage $V_s(t)$ via an LVDT, rotary encoder, or linear potentiometer.
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifies, offsets, and filters $V_s(t)$ to match the microcontroller ADC input range (typically **0 – 5 V** or **0 – 3.3 V**).
4. **Microcontroller (Firmware PID):** Samples $V_s$ via the ADC, computes $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$, executes the discrete PID equation, and outputs a PWM duty cycle.
5. **Power Stage + Actuator:** A motor driver (H-bridge such as L298N, BTS7960, or DRV8871) drives a DC motor, servo, or stepper, which in turn moves the mechanical load and changes $y(t)$.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Sampling:** At instant $kT_s$, the firmware reads the ADC channel connected to the position sensor. If the ADC resolution is $N$ bits, the digital code is $D = \lfloor V_s / V_{ref} \times 2^N \rfloor$.
- **Step 2 — Engineering Units Conversion:** The raw code $D$ is mapped to engineering units (mm or degrees) using the calibration relation $y = a \cdot D + b$, where $a$ is the slope (mm/code) and $b$ is the offset.
- **Step 3 — Error Calculation:** The signed error $e(k) = SP - y(k)$ is formed. This is a **two's complement integer** in the firmware.
- **Step 4 — PID Computation:** Each term is computed independently. Special care is taken to prevent **integral wind-up** (saturation of the integral accumulator) and to filter the derivative term.
- **Step 5 — Saturation Check:** The control output $u(k)$ is clamped between the actuator limits, e.g., $u_{min} = 0$ and $u_{max} = 255$ for an 8-bit PWM register.
- **Step 6 — PWM Update:** The saturated $u(k)$ is written to the timer compare register, generating the next PWM pulse.
- **Step 7 — Loop:** The processor waits until the next sampling tick (typically using a hardware timer interrupt) and the cycle repeats.

## 2.3 Discretisation of the Continuous PID

The continuous PID in the **parallel (ideal) form** is:

$$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Direct digital implementation uses the **backward-difference (Euler) approximation**:

- Integration: $\displaystyle \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$
- Differentiation: $\displaystyle \frac{de}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$

Substituting yields the **positional (absolute) PID algorithm** already given in Section 1.1. A computationally lighter variant is the **velocity (incremental) PID**:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + K_i\,T_s\,e(k) + K_d\,\frac{e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)}{T_s}$$

The microcontroller then updates: $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$.

> [!TIP]
> The **velocity form** is intrinsically **bumpless** during setpoint changes and is the KTU-preferred implementation for actuator-saturated plants.

## 2.4 Stability and Tuning Rules

The **Z-transform** of the discrete PID yields a third-order closed-loop denominator. For KTU purposes, three tuning heuristics are universally accepted:

1. **Ziegler–Nichols Ultimate Gain Method:** Increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillations appear. Record the **ultimate gain $K_u$** and **oscillation period $T_u$**, then apply the lookup table.
2. **Cohen–Coon Method:** Best for first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) models. Yields fast response for setpoint tracking.
3. **Tyreus–Luyben / ITAE Tuning:** Produces the most robust, minimum-overshoot response — the gold standard for displacement control.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Position error | $e(k) = SP - y(k)$ | mm or deg | Direct error input to PID |
| 2 | Proportional term | $P(k) = K_p \, e(k)$ | PWM counts (0–255) | Immediate corrective action |
| 3 | Integral term (rectangular) | $I(k) = K_i T_s \sum e(j)$ | PWM counts | Eliminates steady-state offset |
| 4 | Derivative term (1st-order difference) | $D(k) = \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr]$ | PWM counts | Damps oscillation, anticipates error |
| 5 | Velocity-form update | $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$ | PWM counts | Anti-windup friendly |
| 6 | ADC to engineering units | $y = a \cdot D + b$ | mm or deg | Sensor calibration linearisation |
| 7 | LVDT sensitivity | $K_{LVDT} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{\Delta x}$ | V/mm | Sensor selection |
| 8 | Encoder resolution | $R = \dfrac{360}{CPR \times 4}$ | deg/edge | Quadrature decoding |
| 9 | IAE index | $\int \vert e(t) \vert \,dt$ | mm·s | Aggregate absolute error |
| 10 | ITAE index | $\int t\,\vert e(t) \vert \,dt$ | mm·s² | Time-weighted error (preferred) |
| 11 | Ziegler–Nichols PID gains | $K_p = 0.6 K_u, \; T_i = 0.5 T_u, \; T_d = 0.125 T_u$ | – | Quick empirical tuning |
| 12 | Sampling inequality (Nyquist + factor 10) | $T_s \leq \dfrac{T_{settling}}{10}$ | s | Prevents aliasing |
| 13 | Anti-windup clamp | $\displaystyle I_{clamp} = \dfrac{u_{max} - P - D}{K_i}$ | – | Prevents integrator saturation |
| 14 | Derivative filter (1st order) | $D_f(s) = \dfrac{N K_d s}{1 + s\,K_d/N}$ | – | Limits high-frequency noise gain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-windup** is a **mandatory** sub-topic in the KTU ESE marking scheme. Failure to discuss clamping or back-calculation costs **2–3 marks** out of 14.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microcontroller-based displacement controllers are ubiquitous:

- **CNC machines** and 3D printers (stepper + lead-screw).
- **Robotic manipulator joints** (servo + harmonic drive).
- **Hard-disk drive head positioning** (voice-coil + optical encoder).
- **Aerospace thrust-vector actuators** and satellite antenna pointing.
- **Pick-and-place machines** in PCB assembly.
- **Prosthetic limbs and surgical robots** (high-precision linear actuation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous PID to Discrete Velocity Form

We begin with the **ideal continuous-time PID** acting on the position error $e(t)$:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Express each block in the Laplace domain** (for insight only; the actual implementation is purely discrete):

$$U(s) = \left[K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right] E(s)$$

This is the **transfer function of the ideal PID** and forms the basis for tuning methods such as the Ziegler–Nichols frequency response method.

**Step 2 — Discretise the integrator** using the **backward rectangular rule** (unconditionally stable for any $T_s > 0$):

$$\int_0^{k T_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

This is preferred over the forward (forward-Euler) rule because the backward rule preserves the **BIBO stability** of the original continuous system in most cases. The forward rule maps stable poles of the s-plane into a region **inside the unit circle** of the z-plane only if $T_s$ is small enough, making it conditionally stable.

**Step 3 — Discretise the derivative** using the **first-order backward difference**:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Combine** to obtain the **positional discrete PID**:

$$u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Define the velocity output** by computing $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + K_i T_s e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]$$

**Step 6 — Apply the actuation limits** (saturation function $\text{sat}(\cdot)$):

$$u(k) = \text{sat}\bigl[u(k-1) + \Delta u(k),\; u_{min},\; u_{max}\bigr]$$

This is the firmware-ready expression that the microcontroller executes every $T_s$ seconds via a timer ISR (Interrupt Service Routine).

## 3.2 Derivation: Anti-Windup via Conditional Integration

Let the un-clamped integral be $I_{raw}(k) = I(k-1) + K_i T_s e(k)$. Without clamping, if the actuator is saturated for a long time, $I$ accumulates to huge values, causing a large **overshoot** (the integrator must first "unwind" before it can pull the system back).

The **clamping (conditional integration) anti-windup** rule is:

- Compute the unclamped control: $u_{raw} = P + I_{raw} + D$.
- Apply saturation: $u = \text{sat}(u_{raw}, u_{min}, u_{max})$.
- Define the saturation excess: $e_{sat} = u - u_{raw}$.
- Update the integral **only if the error and the saturation excess have the same sign** (i.e., the system is *not* saturated in the wrong direction):

$$I(k) = \begin{cases} I_{raw}(k) & \text{if } e(k) \cdot e_{sat}(k) \leq 0 \\ I(k-1) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This guarantees that the integrator never accumulates during a saturation event in the offending direction.

## 3.3 Derivation: Derivative on Measurement (Avoid Derivative Kick)

A standard pitfall is taking the derivative of the **error**, which jumps violently when the setpoint changes. The fix is to differentiate the **measurement** $y(k)$ instead:

$$D(k) = -\frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[y(k) - y(k-1)\bigr]$$

Substituting $e(k) = SP - y(k)$ and noting that $SP$ is constant between setpoint changes:

$$D(k) = \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] - \frac{K_d}{T_s}\Delta SP$$

When the setpoint steps, $\Delta SP$ is non-zero, and the derivative term suddenly spikes — this is the **derivative kick**. Differentiating the measurement cancels this spike because $y(k)$ cannot jump instantaneously (the actuator is band-limited).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Problem:** A DC motor–leadscrew rig has a plant transfer function

$$G_p(s) = \frac{50}{s(s + 5)}$$

The position sensor has sensitivity $K_{LVDT} = 0.2$ V/mm and is connected to a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5$ V. The PWM stage has 8-bit resolution (0–255). Compute the digital PID gains using Ziegler–Nichols ultimate gain method, and verify steady-state error for a 25 mm step input.

**Step 1 — Find ultimate gain $K_u$:** The open-loop plant $G_p(s)$ is a 2nd-order type-1 system. With proportional control $C(s) = K_p$, the characteristic equation is $s^2 + 5s + 50 K_p = 0$. Setting $\zeta = 0$ (marginal stability) requires $5 = 0$ which is impossible, so the plant is **already stable** for any $K_p > 0$. In practice, one must include the delay introduced by $T_s$ to find $K_u$, or use the **relay-feedback auto-tuning** method.

For this example, assume we experimentally found $K_u = 4.0$ and $T_u = 1.2$ s.

**Step 2 — Apply Ziegler–Nichols PID rules:**

$$K_p = 0.6 K_u = 0.6 \times 4.0 = 2.4$$

$$T_i = 0.5 T_u = 0.5 \times 1.2 = 0.6 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{2.4}{0.6} = 4.0$$

$$T_d = 0.125 T_u = 0.125 \times 1.2 = 0.15 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_d = K_p T_d = 2.4 \times 0.15 = 0.36$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state error for a step input:** The plant is type 1, so even with proportional-only control the steady-state error is zero. With PI action, the integral term guarantees $e_{ss} = 0$ for any step input, and the displacement will converge exactly to 25 mm.

**Step 4 — Sensor scaling to ADC counts:** $D = V_s / V_{ref} \times 1024 = (0.2 \times y) / 5 \times 1024 = 40.96 \, y$. So **1 mm = 41 ADC counts**, and the **measurement quantum** is approximately **0.0244 mm** — about ten times smaller than typical industrial tolerance.

**Step 5 — Output saturation check:** Maximum control = 255 PWM counts. With $K_p = 2.4$, a 25 mm step gives $P(0) = 60$ counts, $D(0) = 0$, and $I(0) = 0$ at $k=0$. Thus $u(0) = 60$, which is **within saturation** — no wind-up occurs at start.

> [!TIP]
> Always state in your KTU answer: **"The discrete-time PID was implemented with a sampling period of $T_s$ chosen to satisfy the Nyquist criterion relative to the plant's closed-loop bandwidth."** This single sentence fetches full CO marks.

## 3.5 Validation Procedure (Mandatory Lab Steps)

1. **Open-loop step test** — Record plant time constant $\tau$ and DC gain $K$.
2. **Identify FOPDT parameters** — $K$, $L$ (dead time), $T$ (time constant) via the 28.3%/63.2% method or area method.
3. **Compute initial PID gains** using Cohen–Coon or Ziegler–Nichols.
4. **Implement velocity-form PID** in firmware with anti-windup and derivative filtering.
5. **Tune experimentally** by adjusting gains and observing response.
6. **Record performance indices:** rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$ (2% band), overshoot $M_p\%$, steady-state error $e_{ss}$, IAE, ITAE.
7. **Repeat** for setpoint changes and load disturbance tests.
8. **Plot** $y(t)$ vs $r(t)$ and $u(t)$ for documentation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Functional Block Diagram

The diagram below shows the signal flow in a microcontroller-based displacement control loop. Each block represents a functional stage, and arrows indicate unidirectional signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP in mm] --> SUM[Error Comparator]
    YFB[Measured Position y in mm] --> SUM
    SUM[Error Comparator] -->|e k| CTRL[Microcontroller PID Firmware]
    CTRL -->|PWM u k| DRV[Motor Driver H Bridge]
    DRV --> MOT[DC Motor with Gearbox]
    MOT -->|Mechanical Shaft| LOAD[Leadscrew and Slider]
    LOAD -->|Displacement| SENSE[Position Sensor LVDT Encoder]
    SENSE --> SC[Signal Conditioner Amplifier Filter]
    SC -->|Analog Voltage 0 5 V| ADC[Microcontroller ADC]
    ADC -->|Digital Code D k| CTRL
    YFB[Measured Position y in mm]
    LOAD --> SENSE
    SENSE --> SC
    SC --> ADC
    ADC --> CTRL
    CTRL --> SUM
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The outer loop (red path conceptually) carries the physical displacement back to the comparator.
- The inner blue path shows the **firmware** in the microcontroller executing the PID law every $T_s$ seconds.
- The actuator chain is `PWM → Driver → Motor → Leadscrew → Slider`, while the sensor chain is `Slider → Sensor → Conditioner → ADC`.

## 4.2 Firmware State Machine (Discrete-Time Control)

This diagram shows how the microcontroller transitions between its operational states inside the timer ISR.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> IDLE: System Initialised
    IDLE --> SAMPLE: Timer ISR Triggered
    SAMPLE --> COMPUTE: ADC Read Complete
    COMPUTE --> SATURATE: PID Equation Solved
    SATURATE --> OUTPUT: Anti Windup Applied
    OUTPUT --> IDLE: PWM Updated
    COMPUTE --> FAULT: Sensor Out of Range
    FAULT --> IDLE: Error Logged
    IDLE --> [*]: User Stops Loop
```

**State descriptions:**

- **INIT:** Configure ADC, timer, PWM, GPIO. Load $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ from EEPROM.
- **IDLE:** Wait for the next tick of the timer overflow flag.
- **SAMPLE:** Trigger ADC, wait for conversion complete flag, read data register.
- **COMPUTE:** Convert ADC code to engineering units, compute $P$, $I$, $D$, combine into $u_{raw}$.
- **SATURATE:** Apply anti-windup logic, clamp $u$ to PWM limits.
- **OUTPUT:** Write the new duty cycle to the timer compare register.
- **FAULT:** Log error, freeze PWM at safe value, blink status LED.

## 4.3 Signal Conditioning Schematic (LVDT Front End)

```
LVDT Secondary Coils
        |
   +----+----+
   |         |
   |  RECT   |  --> Precision Full-Wave Rectifier (Op-Amp LF353)
   |  FILTER |  --> 2nd-Order Low-Pass (fc = 100 Hz)
   |  AMP    |  --> Non-Inverting Amplifier (Gain = 5)
   +----+----+
        |
        v
    0 - 5 V Analog Signal
        |
        v
   Microcontroller ADC Input Pin
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter** is mandatory to suppress PWM switching noise from the motor driver, which would otherwise inject spurious position readings via the ADC. KTU evaluators specifically look for this noise-mitigation step.

## 4.4 Gain Tuning Map (Heuristic Starting Point)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Open Loop Step Test] --> B[Identify FOPDT Parameters K L T]
    B --> C{Choose Method}
    C -->|Fast Tracking| D[Cohen Coon Tuning]
    C -->|Robust Operation| E[ITAE Tuning]
    C -->|Quick Start| F[Ziegler Nichols]
    D --> G[Set Kp Ki Kd in Firmware]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Run Closed Loop]
    H --> I{Response Acceptable}
    I -->|No| J[Adjust Gains Manually]
    J --> G
    I -->|Yes| K[Record Metrics and Validate]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define a discrete-time PID controller. Why is the velocity form preferred over the positional form in microcontroller implementation?**

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time PID controller is the digital realisation of the continuous PID law, in which the integral is approximated by summation and the derivative by a first-order difference over a fixed sampling period $T_s$. The positional form computes the absolute control output $u(k)$ directly, while the velocity form computes the incremental change $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$.

The velocity form is preferred in microcontroller implementation because:

1. **Bumpless transfer** during setpoint changes — the algorithm naturally avoids sudden jumps in $u$.
2. **Intrinsic anti-windup** — saturation in $u$ is automatically applied at every iteration, preventing integrator overflow.
3. **Simpler arithmetic** — the summation of all past errors is replaced by single-step accumulation of the last integrator value, reducing memory and rounding error.
4. **Easier to port** to actuators that accept increment commands (e.g., stepper pulse trains).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the necessity of anti-windup in a digital PID controller implemented on a microcontroller. State one method to implement it.**

**Model Answer:**

Anti-windup is necessary because the integral term in a PID controller accumulates the error continuously, even when the actuator has reached its physical saturation limit (e.g., PWM duty cycle = 100%). If the error remains non-zero during this saturation period, the integrator builds up a large value. When the setpoint finally becomes achievable, the controller must first "unwind" this accumulated value, causing a severe **overshoot** and possible instability.

**Method — Conditional Integration (Clamping):** Update the integrator only if the control output is not saturated, or if the error and the saturation excess have opposite signs. This prevents the integrator from accumulating further in the wrong direction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the implementation of a closed-loop **linear displacement control system** using a microcontroller. Identify the role of each block and the signals exchanged.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a DC motor–leadscrew position rig, the open-loop transfer function is $G_p(s) = K / [s(T_m s + 1)]$ with $K = 20$ mm/s/V and $T_m = 0.5$ s. Design a digital PID controller using the Ziegler–Nichols ultimate gain method, given that the experimental relay-feedback test produced $K_u = 3.5$ and $T_u = 1.0$ s. The sampling period is $T_s = 0.05$ s. Write the firmware-ready velocity-form update equation.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The block diagram is described in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Each block is functionally explained below:

- **Setpoint generator** issues the desired displacement in mm (e.g., 25 mm). This is a digital value stored in a memory-mapped register.
- **Error comparator** computes $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$. The result is a signed 16-bit integer.
- **Microcontroller** reads $e(k)$ from the ADC, executes the discrete PID law, and outputs a PWM duty cycle.
- **Motor driver (H-bridge)** receives the PWM signal and a direction bit, and supplies a bi-directional current to the **DC motor**.
- **DC motor + gearbox + leadscrew** form the **plant** that converts electrical energy into mechanical translation.
- **Position sensor (LVDT or encoder)** measures the slider position and produces a voltage proportional to displacement.
- **Signal conditioner** amplifies, offsets, and filters the sensor output to the ADC's input range.

The signals exchanged are: **PWM (digital, 0–255 counts), direction (digital 0/1), sensor voltage (analog, 0–5 V), ADC code (digital, 0–1023 counts)**.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply Ziegler–Nichols PID tuning rules** (parallel form):

$$K_p = 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 3.5 = 2.1$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \, T_u = 0.5 \times 1.0 = 0.5 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{2.1}{0.5} = 4.2$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \, T_u = 0.125 \times 1.0 = 0.125 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_d = K_p \, T_d = 2.1 \times 0.125 = 0.2625$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the velocity-form PID** (derived in Section 3.1):

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + K_i T_s e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]$$

Plugging numerical values with $T_s = 0.05$ s:

$$\Delta u(k) = 2.1\,[e(k) - e(k-1)] + (4.2)(0.05)\,e(k) + \frac{0.2625}{0.05}\,[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)]$$

Simplifying:

$$\Delta u(k) = 2.1\,e(k) - 2.1\,e(k-1) + 0.21\,e(k) + 5.25\,e(k) - 10.5\,e(k-1) + 5.25\,e(k-2)$$

$$\Delta u(k) = 7.56\,e(k) - 12.6\,e(k-1) + 5.25\,e(k-2)$$

**Step 3 — Firmware update equation with saturation:**

$$u(k) = \text{sat}\bigl[u(k-1) + 7.56\,e(k) - 12.6\,e(k-1) + 5.25\,e(k-2),\; 0,\; 255\bigr]$$

This is the **complete firmware expression** to be coded inside a timer ISR running at 20 Hz.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the issues that arise when the **derivative term** in a digital PID is naively implemented on raw error. Explain the technique of **derivative-on-measurement** and **first-order derivative filtering** as remedies.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An experimental displacement rig uses a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5$ V and an LVDT with sensitivity $0.25$ V/mm. The setpoint is changed from 0 to 30 mm in a step. Determine (i) the corresponding ADC count change, (ii) the smallest measurable displacement, and (iii) the LSB in engineering units.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Issues with naive derivative on error:**

When the setpoint steps, $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$ suddenly jumps to a large value because $r$ changes instantly. The derivative of this step is an **impulse** (infinite in the limit), which the firmware approximates as a single-sample spike equal to $K_d/T_s \cdot \Delta r$. This phenomenon is called **derivative kick**. It produces:

- A large sudden spike in the control output, often saturating the actuator.
- Mechanical stress and audible noise in the motor.
- Possible triggering of safety cut-offs or driver faults.

**Remedy 1 — Derivative on Measurement:**
Differentiate the **measurement** $y(k)$ rather than the error. Since $y(k)$ cannot jump instantaneously (the plant is band-limited), the derivative spike disappears entirely:

$$D(k) = -\frac{K_d}{T_s}\bigl[y(k) - y(k-1)\bigr]$$

This is mathematically equivalent to differentiating the error, **provided the setpoint is constant**. When the setpoint steps, the term $K_d/T_s \cdot \Delta r$ is replaced by $K_d/T_s \cdot \Delta y$, which is small.

**Remedy 2 — First-Order Derivative Filter (Band-Limited Derivative):**

A pure differentiator has infinite gain at high frequency, which amplifies ADC noise. The standard fix is to low-pass-filter the derivative using the transfer function:

$$D_f(s) = \frac{N K_d s}{1 + s\,K_d/N}$$

where $N$ is the **filter coefficient** (typically 3 to 20). In discrete form, with $T_s$:

$$D_f(k) = \frac{N}{N + T_s/K_d}\,D_f(k-1) + \frac{N K_d}{N T_s + K_d}\,\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr]$$

A larger $N$ makes the filter **wider** (less aggressive, more noise suppression), while a smaller $N$ makes the derivative **sharper** (more responsive, more noisy).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $N = 10$ bits, so $2^N = 1024$. $V_{ref} = 5$ V. $K_{LVDT} = 0.25$ V/mm. Setpoint step $\Delta y = 30$ mm.

**(i) ADC count change corresponding to 30 mm:**

Voltage at full scale: $V_{fs} = K_{LVDT} \times \Delta y = 0.25 \times 30 = 7.5$ V.

This **exceeds** $V_{ref} = 5$ V, meaning the signal would saturate the ADC. A signal conditioner with gain $G = 5/7.5 = 0.667$ is required. With conditioning:

$$D = \frac{V_{fs}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^N = \frac{5}{5} \times 1024 = 1024 \text{ counts (saturated)}$$

**Or, more realistically**, re-scaling with the chosen $G = 0.667$:

$$D = \frac{0.667 \times 7.5}{5} \times 1024 = 1024 \text{ counts}$$

For an unscaled scenario where 30 mm maps to 7.5 V and the ADC is saturated, the **effective** digital span (with appropriate front-end attenuation) is 1024 counts for 30 mm.

**(ii) Smallest measurable displacement (1 ADC count):**

$$\Delta y_{LSB} = \frac{1}{K_{LVDT} \times 2^N / V_{ref}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{K_{LVDT} \times 2^N} = \frac{5}{0.25 \times 1024} = 0.01953 \text{ mm} \approx 19.5 \,\mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Engineering units per LSB:**

This is the same as (ii): **0.0195 mm/LSB** (or 19.5 µm/LSB).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. Students commonly forget to include the **anti-windup** discussion in the PID implementation question and lose **2–3 marks**. Always allocate at least 3–4 lines to this in your answer.
> 2. Many students compute the ADC counts without checking if the **sensor voltage exceeds $V_{ref}$** — KTU examiners love to set this as a trap. Always state whether saturation occurs and how you would scale the signal conditioner.
> 3. Failure to convert $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ into a **single firmware-ready update equation** (as in Q3(b) above) costs **2 marks** even if all the theory is correct.
> 4. **Derivative kick** is a high-frequency question. Mentioning "derivative on measurement" alone, without giving the modified equation, fetches only partial credit. Always write the actual formula.
> 5. Students often confuse the **encoder CPR (counts per revolution)** with the **post-quadrature edge count** (= 4 × CPR). The KTU model answer requires both terms to be mentioned explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Microcontroller-based displacement control** is the discrete-time implementation of a PID regulator on a position plant, with a sensor and actuator chain.
- The **continuous PID** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ becomes the **discrete PID** $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum e(j) + (K_d/T_s)[e(k)-e(k-1)]$.
- The **velocity form** $\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k)-e(k-1)] + K_i T_s e(k) + (K_d/T_s)[e(k)-2e(k-1)+e(k-2)]$ is the **firmware-preferred** form because of anti-windup benefits.
- **Anti-windup** is **mandatory** in KTU answers; clamping or back-calculation methods both earn full marks.
- **Derivative kick** is avoided by **differentiating the measurement**, not the error. **Derivative filtering** (band-limited differentiator with coefficient $N$) suppresses high-frequency noise.
- **ADC resolution** determines the smallest measurable displacement: $\Delta y_{LSB} = V_{ref} / (K_{sensor} \times 2^N)$.
- **Sampling period** must satisfy $T_s \leq T_{settling}/10$ (rule of thumb) and the **Nyquist criterion** relative to the plant bandwidth.
- **Ziegler–Nichols PID rules** (parallel form): $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- **Validation metrics** to record: rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$ (2% band), overshoot $M_p\%$, steady-state error $e_{ss}$, IAE, ITAE.
- **Hardware essentials:** L298N / BTS7960 motor driver, **op-amp** signal conditioner (LF353 or similar), 2nd-order low-pass filter for noise rejection, **timer ISR** for deterministic sampling.
- **Coding essentials:** Use `volatile` for shared variables, disable global interrupts during multi-byte ADC reads, and use **fixed-point arithmetic** (not floating-point) on 8-bit MCUs for speed.
- **Plant identification** via open-loop step test (FOPDT: $K, L, T$) precedes any tuning — a step students often skip in the lab report.
- **Open-loop → Closed-loop → Tuned → Validated** is the mandatory experiment flow, and the report should contain the step response plot, gain table, and performance index table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Create a program that displays data from the sensor in regular intervals in a compact format
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPL508 - PCCPL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Create a program that displays data from the sensor in regular intervals in a compact format

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Embedded Systems** and **Real-Time Programming** (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCCPL508 - Programming Lab), a program that **"displays data from the sensor in regular intervals in a compact format"** is a structured C application that:

1. **Acquires** analog or digital readings from a peripheral sensor (e.g., **LM35** temperature sensor, **DHT11** humidity sensor, **MPU6050** accelerometer).
2. **Processes** the raw data through a defined sampling interval using hardware timers, software delay loops, or RTOS tick interrupts.
3. **Formats and presents** the data in a **space-optimized, human-readable** form (e.g., `T:24.5C H:60%`) on an output device such as an **LCD**, **Serial Terminal (UART)**, or **Seven-Segment Display**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "compact format" requirement enforces the use of structured I/O functions (`printf`, `sprintf`, `lcd_data`) with carefully chosen format specifiers and field widths — a foundational concept aligned with **Course Outcome CO1** (Apply programming concepts to solve real-world problems) and **CO2** (Design modular embedded applications).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Sampling Interval ($T_s$):**
> The fixed time period (typically in **milliseconds** or **seconds**) between two consecutive sensor readings. Mathematically, if the sampling frequency is $f_s$, then the sampling interval is $T_s = 1/f_s$. The inverse relationship is fundamental to the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$, where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the signal being measured.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are monitoring the **temperature of a refrigerator** using a digital thermometer mounted on the door. You do **not** need to read the temperature every microsecond — that would be wasteful. Instead, you glance at it **once every 5 seconds**, and the LCD shows something like:

```
T: 04 C  H: 62%
```

This is **exactly** what our program does:
- The **sensor** = the thermometer probe.
- The **regular interval** = the 5-second wait between glances.
- The **compact format** = the short, fixed-width string on the LCD.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the program as a meticulous watchman** ⏰: He checks the gauge (sensor), records the value (reads ADC), and writes it on a small whiteboard (LCD) — then waits for a fixed period before checking again. He never over-writes the whole board; he only updates specific characters to keep the display readable and flicker-free.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Computational Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Lab) | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Default Sampling Interval | **2 seconds** | $T_s$ |
| ADC Reference Voltage | **5.0 V** (Arduino UNO) | $V_{ref}$ |
| ADC Resolution | **10-bit (1024 levels)** | $2^{10}$ |
| UART Baud Rate (Serial Display) | **9600 bps** | $B$ |
| LCD Character Width | **16 × 2** (HD44780) | $W \times H$ |
| Compact String Buffer Size | **17 bytes** (16 + null) | $N_{buf}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Always **declare a buffer size one byte larger** than the visible characters to accommodate the **null terminator `\0`** — a common KTU valuation pitfall.

---

## 1.4 Why "Compact Format" Matters

In embedded systems, **every byte of RAM and every cycle of CPU time is precious**. A compact format:
- **Reduces RAM usage** — small buffers mean more memory is free for stack/heap.
- **Speeds up LCD refresh** — fewer characters to write means lower latency.
- **Improves readability** — fixed-width fields align neatly on the LCD.
- **Saves power** — fewer I/O toggles on the data bus.

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly tests the ability to use format specifiers such as `%2d`, `%5.2f`, and `%c` with controlled field widths to achieve this compactness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The complete program execution can be broken into **five structured stages**. Understanding each stage is critical for both lab viva and KTU end-semester examinations.

### Stage 1 — Sensor Initialization (`sensor_init()`)
- Configures the **ADC channel**, sets **reference voltage**, and sets **prescaler bits** in the ADC control register.
- For digital sensors (DHT11, MPU6050), initializes the **I²C** or **single-wire GPIO** protocol.

### Stage 2 — Reading Raw Data (`sensor_read()`)
- Triggers the ADC conversion by setting the **ADSC (ADC Start Conversion)** bit in `ADCSRA`.
- Waits for the **ADIF (ADC Interrupt Flag)** to be set — this is hardware-based polling.
- Returns a digital value between **0 and 1023** (10-bit).

### Stage 3 — Conversion to Engineering Units
The raw ADC value must be mapped to a physical quantity (e.g., °C, %, m/s²). The two principal conversions are:

1. **Voltage Conversion:**
$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

2. **Temperature Conversion (for LM35, 10 mV/°C):**
$$T(°C) = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01}$$

3. **Percentage Conversion (generic 0–5 V sensor):**
$$P(\%) = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 100$$

### Stage 4 — Interval Timing (Polling Delay)
The **regular interval** between samples is implemented using:
- **Software delay loops** (`_delay_ms()` from `util/delay.h` in AVR-GCC).
- **Hardware Timer Interrupts** (more accurate, RTOS-friendly).
- **Arduino `delay()` / `millis()`** functions.

### Stage 5 — Compact Formatting & Display
The processed sensor value is converted to a fixed-width string using `sprintf()` and written to the LCD or UART. Example:

```c
sprintf(buf, "T:%2d.%1dC", int_part, frac_part);
lcd_puts(buf);
```

---

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

| Step | Why It Is Required | How It Is Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Initialization | Ensures deterministic behaviour at startup | Function call in `main()` before loop |
| Reading | Captures physical world into digital domain | `ADC` register access or `analogRead()` |
| Conversion | Maps raw counts to engineering units | Linear scaling formulas |
| Delay | Enforces "regular interval" requirement | `_delay_ms(T_s * 1000)` |
| Formatting | Achieves "compact format" requirement | `sprintf` with `%2d`, `%5.1f` |
| Display | Human-readable output | LCD `lcd_gotoxy()` + `lcd_puts()` |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your one-stop reference for the KTU 2024 ESE on this experiment. Memorize the formulas and the unit conventions.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Interval | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | $T_s$ in **seconds**, $f_s$ in **Hz** |
| Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_{max}$ = max signal frequency |
| ADC Voltage | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | $V_{in}, V_{ref}$ in **Volts** |
| Resolution (LSB) | $LSB = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | $LSB$ in **V/count** |
| LM35 Temperature | $T = \dfrac{V_{in}}{0.01}$ | $T$ in **°C** |
| Percentage Output | $P = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 100$ | $P$ in **%** |
| Integer-Fraction Split | $T_{int} = \lfloor T \rfloor$, $T_{frac} = \lfloor (T - T_{int}) \cdot 10 \rfloor$ | Used for **fixed-point display** |
| UART Time per Byte | $t_{byte} = \dfrac{10}{B}$ | $t_{byte}$ in **seconds**, $B$ in **bps** |
| Total Display Refresh | $t_{refresh} = N_{chars} \cdot t_{byte}$ | For serial displays |
| Timer Overflow (8-bit) | $T_{ovf} = \dfrac{256 \cdot Prescaler}{F_{CPU}}$ | $T_{ovf}$ in **seconds** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** When writing inline math within markdown table cells, the pipe `|` symbol is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. For example, $\vert V_{in} \vert$ is correct, but `$|V_{in}|$` will corrupt the table rendering.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

This exact pattern — **periodic sensor sampling with compact display** — is the backbone of:

1. 🏥 **Patient Vital Monitors** — Heart rate & SpO₂ sampled every 1 s, displayed on a 7-segment panel.
2. 🏭 **Industrial PLCs** — Pressure and temperature updated every 500 ms on HMI screens.
3. 🌡️ **Smart HVAC Systems** — Indoor climate controlled by sensor feedback every 2–5 s.
4. 🛰️ **Satellite Telemetry** — Sensor data beaconed to ground stations at fixed intervals.
5. 🚗 **Automotive Dashboards** — Speed, RPM, and fuel level updated at 10 Hz refresh rate.
6. 🏠 **IoT Weather Stations** — Compact JSON or CSV payload transmitted every minute.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Interview Tip:** When asked *"Why not sample continuously?"* in viva, answer: *"Continuous sampling wastes CPU cycles, increases power consumption, may cause display flicker, and generates redundant data. Periodic sampling with a suitable interval following the Nyquist theorem is the embedded design best practice."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC to Voltage Conversion

The **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** discretizes a continuous analog voltage $V_{in}$ into a binary number $D$ of $n$ bits.

**Step 1:** The ADC divides the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ into $2^n - 1$ equal quantization levels (since 0 is also a valid code).

$$\text{Number of levels} = 2^n - 1$$

**Step 2:** Each level (Least Significant Bit) corresponds to a fixed voltage step:

$$LSB = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 3:** The output digital code $D$ is proportional to the input voltage:

$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{LSB} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot (2^n - 1)}{V_{ref}}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $V_{in}$:

$$\boxed{V_{in} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}}$$

**Example (KTU Lab Scenario):** An LM35 output of `0.245 V` is read by a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0 \, V$.

$$D = \frac{0.245 \cdot 1023}{5.0} = 50.127 \approx 50$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Code to Temperature (LM35)

The **LM35** sensor outputs a linearly scaled voltage: **10 mV per °C**.

**Step 1:** Establish the sensor transfer function:

$$V_{out} = 0.01 \cdot T \quad \text{(T in °C)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the ADC voltage relation:

$$0.01 \cdot T = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $T$:

$$T = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{0.01 \cdot (2^n - 1)}$$

**Step 4:** Plug in numerical values $V_{ref} = 5.0 \, V$, $n = 10$:

$$T = \frac{D \cdot 5.0}{0.01 \cdot 1023} = \frac{D \cdot 5.0}{10.23}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx \frac{D}{2.046} \quad \text{(°C)}}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $D = 512$:

$$T = \frac{512}{2.046} \approx 25.01 \, °C$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sampling Interval from Timer Prescaler

For an **AVR ATmega328P** running at $F_{CPU} = 16 \, \text{MHz}$ with Timer1 in Normal Mode:

**Step 1:** The timer increments at a rate of:

$$f_{timer} = \frac{F_{CPU}}{Prescaler}$$

**Step 2:** An 8-bit timer overflows when it counts from 0 to 255 (256 counts):

$$T_{ovf} = \frac{256}{f_{timer}} = \frac{256 \cdot Prescaler}{F_{CPU}}$$

**Step 3:** For a prescaler of 1024:

$$T_{ovf} = \frac{256 \cdot 1024}{16 \times 10^6} = \frac{262144}{16 \times 10^6} = 0.016384 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 4:** To achieve a 2-second interval, the number of overflows required is:

$$N = \frac{2.0}{0.016384} \approx 122.07$$

Rounding up: $N = 123$ overflows.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fixed-Point Display Split

A floating-point value like `25.4` cannot be displayed directly using integer `sprintf` in some embedded compilers. We split it into **integer** and **fractional** parts:

**Step 1:** Extract integer part:

$$T_{int} = \lfloor T \rfloor = 25$$

**Step 2:** Subtract integer part from original:

$$T_{rem} = T - T_{int} = 0.4$$

**Step 3:** Scale remainder to single digit:

$$T_{frac} = \lfloor T_{rem} \cdot 10 \rfloor = 4$$

**Step 4:** Format using `sprintf`:

```c
sprintf(buf, "T:%2d.%1dC", T_int, T_frac);
```

**Output:** `T:25.4C` — compact, fixed-width, and LCD-friendly.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Display Refresh Time (UART)

For serial display at baud rate $B = 9600 \, \text{bps}$:

**Step 1:** Each UART frame = 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop = **10 bits**.

$$t_{bit} = \frac{1}{9600} = 104.17 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2:** Time to send one character:

$$t_{char} = 10 \cdot t_{bit} = 1.0417 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 3:** To transmit a 16-character compact string (e.g., `T:25.4C H:60.0% `):

$$t_{refresh} = 16 \cdot 1.0417 = 16.67 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum refresh rate:

$$f_{refresh} = \frac{1}{0.01667} \approx 60 \, \text{Hz}$$

Since our sampling interval is 2 s ≫ 16.67 ms, the display has ample time to refresh.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Interval

| Application | Required $f_{max}$ | Min $f_s$ (Nyquist) | Practical $f_s$ | Practical $T_s$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Room temperature | 0.1 Hz | 0.2 Hz | 0.5 Hz | **2 s** |
| Heart rate (ECG) | 40 Hz | 80 Hz | 250 Hz | 4 ms |
| Audio (voice) | 4 kHz | 8 kHz | 44.1 kHz | 22.7 µs |
| Vibration monitoring | 500 Hz | 1 kHz | 5 kHz | 200 µs |
| Soil moisture | 0.01 Hz | 0.02 Hz | 0.1 Hz | 10 s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

The following diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **raw uppercase labels** inside double quotes — no markdown bold or HTML tables inside node text.

---

## 4.3 Program Flow Chart — Main Loop

```mermaid
    stepB["INITIALIZE LCD UART ADC"]:::init

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepD

    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

---

## 4.4 Function Call Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mainNode["MAIN FUNCTION"]:::main
    initNode["SYSTEM_INIT"]:::func
    lcdNode["LCD_INIT"]:::func
    adcNode["ADC_INIT"]:::func
    uartNode["UART_INIT"]:::func
    readNode["SENSOR_READ"]:::func
    convNode["VOLTAGE_CONVERT"]:::func
    tempNode["TEMP_CONVERT"]:::func
    fmtNode["FORMAT_DISPLAY"]:::func
    showNode["LCD_SHOW"]:::func
    serialNode["SERIAL_SHOW"]:::func

    mainNode --> initNode
    initNode --> lcdNode
    initNode --> adcNode
    initNode --> uartNode
    mainNode --> readNode
    readNode --> convNode
    convNode --> tempNode

    mainNode --> showNode
    mainNode --> serialNode
    fmtNode --> showNode
    fmtNode --> serialNode

    classDef main fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef func fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.5 Reference Implementation (Arduino/C Framework)

```c
/*
 * Program: Display sensor data in regular intervals in a compact format
 * Board:   Arduino UNO (ATmega328P)
 * Sensor:  LM35 (Temperature)
 * Output:  16x2 LCD + Serial Monitor (9600 bps)
 * Course:  PCCPL508 - KTU 2024 Scheme
 */

#include <LiquidCrystal.h>

LiquidCrystal lcd(12, 11, 5, 4, 3, 2);

const int SENSOR_PIN = A0;
const float VREF      = 5.0;
const int   ADC_BITS  = 10;
const int   INTERVAL  = 2000;   // 2 seconds
char buf[17];                   // 16 chars + null

void setup() {
    Serial.begin(9600);
    lcd.begin(16, 2);
    lcd.print("Sensor Ready...");
    delay(1000);
    lcd.clear();
}

void loop() {
    int raw = analogRead(SENSOR_PIN);
    float voltage = (raw * VREF) / (pow(2, ADC_BITS) - 1);
    float tempC   = voltage / 0.01;
    int   tInt    = (int)tempC;
    int   tFrac   = (int)((tempC - tInt) * 10);

    sprintf(buf, "T:%2d.%1dC", tInt, tFrac);

    lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
    lcd.print(buf);
    lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
    lcd.print("Status: OK");

    Serial.print("Reading: ");
    Serial.println(buf);

    delay(INTERVAL);
}
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

### **Q1. Define "sampling interval" in the context of sensor-based embedded systems. What is its relationship with the Nyquist sampling theorem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **sampling interval** ($T_s$) is the fixed time period (usually in seconds) between two consecutive readings of a sensor. It is the reciprocal of the **sampling frequency** ($f_s$):

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that to accurately reconstruct a signal, the sampling frequency must be at least **twice the maximum frequency component** present in the signal:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad T_s \leq \frac{1}{2 \cdot f_{max}}$$

For a room temperature sensor where $f_{max} \approx 0.1 \, \text{Hz}$, we choose $T_s = 2 \, \text{s}$ (i.e., $f_s = 0.5 \, \text{Hz}$), which comfortably satisfies the Nyquist criterion.

---

### **Q2. List any three techniques used in C to display sensor data in a "compact format" on an LCD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Fixed-width format specifiers** in `sprintf()` — e.g., `%2d` (two-digit zero-padded integer) and `%5.2f` (5-character wide float with 2 decimals).
2. **Pre-allocated character buffer** of exact size (e.g., `char buf[17]`) and use of `\0` null terminator.
3. **Tag-based abbreviation** — using short labels like `T:` for temperature and `H:` for humidity to save characters.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2]**

### ✦ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Design a C program for an **ATmega328P** microcontroller that reads the **ambient temperature** from an **LM35 sensor** connected to ADC channel **A0**, converts it to **°C**, and displays the value in the compact format **`T:XX.YC`** on a **16×2 LCD** every **2 seconds**. Show all function definitions and the main loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the program in (a), derive the mathematical formula used to convert the **raw ADC value** to temperature, assuming a 5 V reference and 10-bit resolution. Calculate the displayed value when the raw ADC output is **205**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Program Design (7 marks):**

```c
#include <avr/io.h>
#include <util/delay.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include "lcd.h"   // Custom LCD header for HD44780

#define SENSOR_PIN    0       // ADC0
#define INTERVAL_MS   2000
#define VREF          5.0
#define ADC_MAX       1023.0

void adc_init(void) {
    ADMUX  = (1 << REFS0);                     // AVcc reference
    ADCSRA = (1 << ADEN)  | (1 << ADPS2) |
             (1 << ADPS1) | (1 << ADPS0);      // Enable, prescaler 128
}

uint16_t adc_read(uint8_t ch) {
    ch &= 0x07;
    ADMUX = (ADMUX & 0xF8) | ch;
    ADCSRA |= (1 << ADSC);
    while (ADCSRA & (1 << ADSC));
    return ADC;
}

float adc_to_voltage(uint16_t adc) {
    return (adc * VREF) / ADC_MAX;
}

float voltage_to_temp(float v) {
    return v / 0.01;      // LM35: 10 mV/°C
}

void format_compact(char *buf, float t) {
    int tInt  = (int)t;
    int tFrac = (int)((t - tInt) * 10);
    sprintf(buf, "T:%2d.%1dC", tInt, tFrac);
}

int main(void) {
    char buf[17];
    lcd_init();
    adc_init();
    lcd_print("Sensor Ready");
    _delay_ms(1000);
    lcd_clear();

    while (1) {
        uint16_t raw  = adc_read(SENSOR_PIN);
        float     v   = adc_to_voltage(raw);
        float     t   = voltage_to_temp(v);
        format_compact(buf, t);

        lcd_gotoxy(0, 0);
        lcd_puts(buf);
        lcd_gotoxy(0, 1);
        lcd_puts("Refreshing...");

        _delay_ms(INTERVAL_MS);
        lcd_gotoxy(0, 1);
        lcd_puts("              ");
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

- `adc_init()`: Configures the ADC with **AVcc as reference** and **prescaler 128** (to bring the 16 MHz clock down to 125 kHz, within the recommended 50–200 kHz ADC clock range).
- `adc_read()`: Selects channel by masking `ADMUX`, starts conversion, waits for completion, returns the 10-bit value.
- `format_compact()`: Splits the float into integer and fractional parts and packs them into a 7-character string including the null terminator.
- The main `while(1)` loop polls every 2 seconds, satisfying the **"regular interval"** requirement.

---

**(b) Derivation & Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Voltage from ADC:**

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{ADC \cdot 5.0}{1023}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature from Voltage (LM35):**

$$T = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01} = \frac{ADC \cdot 5.0}{1023 \cdot 0.01} = \frac{ADC \cdot 5.0}{10.23} = \frac{ADC}{2.046}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $ADC = 205$:**

$$T = \frac{205}{2.046} = 100.19 \, °C$$

Wait — that is unusually high. Let us verify the **integer–fractional split**:

$$T_{int}  = \lfloor 100.19 \rfloor = 100$$
$$T_{rem}  = 100.19 - 100 = 0.19$$
$$T_{frac} = \lfloor 0.19 \cdot 10 \rfloor = 1$$

**Step 4 — Compact format string:**

$$\texttt{sprintf(buf, "T:\%2d.\%1dC", 100, 1)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \texttt{buf = "T:100.1C"}$$

⚠ **Note:** Since `%2d` only guarantees a **minimum** width of 2, the value 100 expands naturally to 3 digits, which still fits within the 16-character LCD row. The display reads: **`T:100.1C`**.

---

### ✦ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** with its mathematical statement. Justify why a **2-second sampling interval** is appropriate for a **room temperature monitoring system** but inadequate for an **ECG (electrocardiogram) signal**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program snippet to read a **DHT11 humidity sensor** (digital) and display both **temperature and humidity** in the compact format **`T:XXC H:XX%`** on the Serial Monitor every **3 seconds**. Use the `DHT.h` library and show only the relevant loop section. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Nyquist Theorem Justification (7 marks):**

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that a bandlimited continuous signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Equivalently, the sampling interval must satisfy:

$$T_s \leq \frac{1}{2 \cdot f_{max}}$$

**Case 1 — Room Temperature Monitoring:**

Room temperature changes very slowly. The dominant thermal time constant of a building is on the order of **minutes to hours**, so:

$$f_{max} \approx 0.01 \, \text{Hz} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_s \leq 50 \, \text{s}$$

A **2-second interval** gives $f_s = 0.5 \, \text{Hz}$, which is **25× the Nyquist minimum** — comfortably adequate.

**Case 2 — ECG Signal:**

The ECG waveform has clinically significant frequency components up to **150 Hz** (QRS complex). Applying Nyquist:

$$f_s \geq 300 \, \text{Hz} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_s \leq 3.33 \, \text{ms}$$

A 2-second interval gives $f_s = 0.5 \, \text{Hz}$ — **600× too low**, completely missing the cardiac waveform. This would be **clinically useless and dangerous**.

**Conclusion:** The choice of sampling interval is **application-dependent** and must be derived from the highest frequency content of interest in the signal.

---

**(b) DHT11 Program (7 marks):**

```c
#include "DHT.h"

#define DHTPIN    2
#define DHTTYPE   DHT11
#define INTERVAL  3000

DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
char buf[17];

void setup() {
    Serial.begin(9600);
    dht.begin();
    Serial.println("DHT11 Sensor Ready");
}

void loop() {
    float h = dht.readHumidity();
    float t = dht.readTemperature();

    if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) {
        Serial.println("Sensor Error!");
        delay(INTERVAL);
        return;
    }

    int hInt = (int)h;
    int tInt = (int)t;
    int hFrac = (int)((h - hInt) * 10);
    int tFrac = (int)((t - tInt) * 10);

    sprintf(buf, "T:%2d.%1dC H:%2d.%1d%%", tInt, tFrac, hInt, hFrac);

    Serial.print("Reading: ");
    Serial.println(buf);

    delay(INTERVAL);
}
```

**Explanation of Key Points:**

- `dht.begin()` initializes the single-wire GPIO protocol.
- `isnan()` check guards against sensor read failures (returns `NaN` if checksum fails).
- The `%%` in `sprintf` escapes the percent sign, which is required because `%` is the format specifier trigger in C.
- The 3-second interval matches the **DHT11 datasheet minimum sampling period** of 1 second plus safety margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting `%%` in `sprintf`** when printing a literal percent sign — the compiler will treat the next character as a format specifier and may **crash or print garbage**. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 2. **Buffer size mismatch** — declaring `char buf[7]` for an 8-character string (including `\0`) causes **memory overrun** and undefined behaviour. Always allocate `N + 1` bytes.
> 3. **Confusing `%d` with `%f`** — using `%d` for a float silently truncates the decimal. Use fixed-point split or cast properly.
> 4. **No `isnan()` check** for digital sensors like DHT11 — KTU examiners will deduct marks for missing error handling.
> 5. **Hardcoding the delay** without explaining the relation to Nyquist theorem — the "regular interval" question often asks to **justify the chosen value**, not just implement it.
> 6. **Mixing `lcd_puts()` and `Serial.println()` output** without proper format alignment — leads to inconsistent display.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🔑 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **sampling interval** ($T_s$) is the time gap between two sensor readings; it is the reciprocal of the **sampling frequency** ($f_s$).
- ✅ The **Nyquist Theorem** mandates $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ to prevent aliasing.
- ✅ **ADC voltage formula:** $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$
- ✅ **LM35 temperature formula:** $T = \dfrac{V_{in}}{0.01}$ (output is 10 mV/°C).
- ✅ **Compact format** is achieved using: fixed-width `sprintf` specifiers, tag prefixes (`T:`, `H:`), and integer–fractional splits.
- ✅ **Buffer size rule:** always allocate one extra byte for the **null terminator `\0`**.
- ✅ **Escape `%` in `sprintf`** as `%%` when printing a literal percent character.
- ✅ **Digital sensors** (DHT11, MPU6050) require `isnan()` or checksum validation in C.
- ✅ **Choosing $T_s$:** slow signals (temperature) → seconds; fast signals (ECG) → milliseconds.
- ✅ **Program structure:** `init() → read() → convert() → format() → display() → delay() → loop`.
- ✅ **Buffer overflow protection** is mandatory in KTU lab viva — declare `char buf[17]` for a 16-char LCD.
- ✅ **Polling vs Interrupt:** Polling (software delay) is simpler; Interrupts (hardware timer) are more accurate.
- ✅ **Refresh rate** of a serial display depends on baud rate $B$ and number of characters $N$: $t_{refresh} = \dfrac{10N}{B}$.
- ✅ **Knee-point knowledge:** ATmega328P ADC needs a clock between **50 kHz and 200 kHz** for 10-bit accuracy.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** This experiment primarily tests **CO1** (problem solving), **CO2** (embedded design), and **CO3** (modular C programming).

topics.title: From the available sensors and actuators, find or create code that will display the sensed data on the PC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPL508 - PCCPL508 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** From the available sensors and actuators, find or create code that will display the sensed data on the PC # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** and **IoT-enabled embedded labs**, the process of *displaying sensed data on a PC* is formally defined as the **Data Acquisition, Serial Bridging, and Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Rendering Pipeline**. It involves reading an analog or digital quantity from a physical transducer (sensor), converting it into a standardized digital protocol frame, transmitting it over a wired (USB/UART) or wireless (Bluetooth/Wi-Fi) link, and finally rendering it on a host computer using a terminal emulator, GUI dashboard, or web browser. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Syllabus Terminology** > - **Sensor** – A transducer that converts a physical parameter (temperature, light, distance) into an electrical signal. > - **Actuator** – A device that converts an electrical command into physical action (LED, motor, relay, buzzer). > - **Serial Monitor** – A text-stream console (Arduino IDE / PuTTY / Tera Term) used to visualize `\r\n` terminated ASCII frames. > - **Baud Rate** – Symbols per second on a UART line. Standard KTU lab value = **9600 bps** unless overridden. > - **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** – The software layer on the PC that interprets and visualizes telemetry. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The "Doctor–Stethoscope–Chart" Pipeline** > Think of the sensor as a **stethoscope** listening to a patient's heartbeat, the **microcontroller (Arduino/ESP32)** as the **doctor** interpreting the sound, the **USB cable** as the **phone line** the doctor uses to call the hospital, and the **PC screen (Serial Monitor / Dashboard)** as the **patient's chart** where the heartbeat is plotted over time. > > Just as the doctor must "speak a common language" (English) with the hospital, the microcontroller must "speak" the same **serial protocol** at an agreed **baud rate** with the PC. If the doctor speaks too fast or too slow, the chart looks garbled — exactly what happens when baud rates mismatch on `Serial.begin()`. The data flow chain is: $$ \text{Physical Quantity} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensor}} \text{Analog/Digital Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{ADC/MCU}} \text{Binary Frame} \xrightarrow{\text{UART/USB}} \text{PC Terminal} $$ ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | **Baud Rate (Lab Default)** | **9600** | bits/second (bps) | | **USB-UART Chip (Arduino Uno)** | ATmega16U2 | — | | **Logic Level (TTL)** | **0 V – 5 V** (Uno) / **0 V – 3.3 V** (ESP32) | Volts | | **ADC Resolution (Arduino Uno)** | **10-bit** → 0–1023 | levels | | **ADC Reference Voltage** | **5.0 V** (default) | Volts | | **Serial Frame Format** | 8N1 (8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit) | — | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Data Display Pipeline The complete process of "sensor → PC display" can be decomposed into **five sequential logical stages**. Each stage is mandatory; failure in any one results in a `null`, garbage characters, or a frozen terminal. ### Stage 1 — Physical Transduction The sensor element (e.g., thermistor in DHT11, photodiode in LDR module) converts the measurand $M$ into an electrical quantity $V_{\text{sense}}$. ### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning The raw voltage may be amplified, filtered, or linearized. For digital sensors (DHT11, DS18B20), this stage happens *inside* the sensor IC. For analog sensors (LM35, LDR), the MCU's **ADC** performs sampling. ### Stage 3 — Microcontroller Digitization The MCU samples the analog pin using Successive Approximation Register (SAR) logic: $$ \text{ADC Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{input}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \times (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor $$ where $n = 10$ for Arduino Uno, giving $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$. ### Stage 4 — UART/USB Framing The digital value is converted to **ASCII** characters and framed into a UART packet: - **1 Start bit** (LOW) - **8 Data bits** (LSB first) - **1 Stop bit** (HIGH) - Total = **10 bit-times** per byte ### Stage 5 — PC-Side Rendering The host's CDC (Communications Device Class) driver receives the byte stream and pipes it to the terminal application (Serial Monitor, PuTTY, Python `pyserial`, Processing sketch, or web dashboard). ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage - **Why ASCII?** ASCII is human-readable and terminal-agnostic. Raw binary would require hex viewers. - **Why 8N1?** Universally supported by every USB-UART bridge chip. - **Why baud rate matching?** A baud-rate mismatch of even 2% produces framing errors because the receiver samples each bit at the *center* of the bit-time. - **Why `\r\n` line endings?** Windows terminals historically required carriage return + line feed; modern IDEs accept both. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight** > The single most common viva question is: *"What happens if the baud rate in `Serial.begin()` does not match the Serial Monitor?"* > **Answer:** The monitor displays **mojibake** (garbled characters like `ÿÞªÆ¥`) because each bit is sampled at the wrong instant, shifting the byte boundaries. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ADC to Voltage | $V = \dfrac{\text{ADC} \times V_{\text{ref}}}{1023}$ | Volts (Arduino Uno) | | 2 | LM35 Temperature | $T = V_{\text{out}} \times 100$ | °C (10 mV/°C slope) | | 3 | DHT11 Temperature | $T = \text{byte2} + \text{byte3} \times 0.1$ | °C (protocol-defined) | | 4 | Ultrasonic Distance | $d = \dfrac{t_{\text{echo}} \times 0.0343}{2}$ | cm (speed of sound = **343 m/s**) | | 5 | LDR Lux (approx) | $L = \dfrac{R_{\text{LDR}}}{10}$ (mapped) | lux (qualitative mapping) | | 6 | UART Frame Length | $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{10}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | seconds per byte | | 7 | Total Transmission Time | $T_{\text{total}} = N \times \dfrac{10}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | $N$ = number of bytes | | 8 | ESP32 Wi-Fi Throughput | $R_{\text{wifi}} \leq 150$ | Mbps (802.11n) | | 9 | PySerial Read Formula | `data = ser.readline().decode('utf-8').strip()` | Python `pyserial` | | 10 | CSV Logging Format | `timestamp, sensor_id, value` | Comma-Separated Values | > **Engineering Utility** > This pipeline is the **foundation of every IoT product** — from wearable fitness bands (sensor → BLE → phone) to industrial SCADA systems (sensor → Modbus → HMI dashboard). Mastering the PC display stage is the gateway to cloud telemetry (ThingSpeak, AWS IoT, Blynk). --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — LM35 Temperature Sensor → PC Serial Monitor **Problem Statement:** An LM35 analog temperature sensor is connected to **A0** of an Arduino Uno. Derive the firmware code and PC-side Python script to display the live temperature in °C on the PC terminal every 1 second. ### 3.1.1 Circuit Mathematical Model The LM35 output voltage is linearly proportional to temperature: $$ V_{\text{out}} = 10 \,\text{mV} / °\text{C} \times T $$ The Arduino's ADC maps $0 \le V_{\text{out}} \le 5\,\text{V}$ to integer codes $0 \le \text{ADC} \le 1023$: $$ \text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{5.0} \times 1023 \right\rfloor $$ Substituting $V_{\text{out}} = 0.01 \times T$: $$ \text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{0.01 \times T}{5.0} \times 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 0.002046 \times T \right\rfloor $$ Inverting for $T$: $$ T = \frac{\text{ADC} \times 5.0}{1023 \times 0.01} = \frac{\text{ADC} \times 5.0}{10.23} $$ $$ \boxed{T \approx \frac{\text{ADC}}{2.046} \,\, °\text{C}} $$ ### 3.1.2 Firmware (Arduino Sketch) — Step-by-Step Construction ```cpp // ============================================================ // Experiment 2: Display LM35 Sensor Data on PC // Course: PCCPL508 | KTU 2024 Scheme // ============================================================ // STEP 1: Define the analog input pin const int LM35_PIN = A0; // STEP 2: Define the ADC reference voltage (Arduino Uno default) const float V_REF = 5.0; // STEP 3: Define the LM35 scale factor (10 mV per °C) const float LM35_SCALE = 0.01; // Volts per °C // STEP 4: Define the ADC resolution (10-bit = 1024 levels) const int ADC_MAX = 1023; // STEP 5: Setup routine — runs once on power-up / reset void setup() { // STEP 5a: Initialize serial communication at 9600 bps, 8N1 Serial.begin(9600); // STEP 5b: Wait for the native USB port to be ready (Leonardo/Micro) while (!Serial) { ; } // STEP 5c: Print a friendly header Serial.println("======================================"); Serial.println(" KTU PCCPL508 | LM35 Temperature Logger"); Serial.println(" Format: ADC, Voltage(V), Temp(C)"); Serial.println("======================================"); } // STEP 6: Loop routine — runs forever void loop() { // STEP 6a: Read the raw 10-bit ADC value from the sensor pin int adcValue = analogRead(LM35_PIN); // STEP 6b: Convert ADC code to voltage float voltage = (adcValue * V_REF) / ADC_MAX; // STEP 6c: Convert voltage to temperature using LM35 scale float temperatureC = voltage / LM35_SCALE; // STEP 6d: Transmit formatted ASCII frame over UART Serial.print("ADC: "); Serial.print(adcValue); Serial.print(" | V: "); Serial.print(voltage, 3); // 3 decimal places Serial.print(" V | T: "); Serial.print(temperatureC, 2); // 2 decimal places Serial.println(" C"); // STEP 6e: 1-second delay between samples delay(1000); } ``` **Algebraic Verification of the Code's Internal Math:** The Arduino function `analogRead()` returns: $$ \text{adcValue} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \times 1023 \right\rfloor $$ Rearranging: $$ V_{\text{in}} = \frac{\text{adcValue} \times V_{\text{ref}}}{1023} = \frac{\text{adcValue} \times 5.0}{1023} $$ Temperature in °C: $$ T = \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{0.01} = \frac{\text{adcValue} \times 5.0}{1023 \times 0.01} \approx \frac{\text{adcValue}}{2.046} $$ For **adcValue = 204** → $T \approx 99.7\,°\text{C}$ ✓ (physically consistent). ### 3.1.3 PC-Side Python Script — `pyserial` Real-Time Plotter ```python # ============================================================ # PC-Side Real-Time Temperature Visualizer # Run: pip install pyserial matplotlib # ============================================================ import serial import re import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from collections import deque from matplotlib.animation import FuncAnimation # STEP 1: Open the virtual COM port (check Device Manager for COMx) SERIAL_PORT = "COM5" # Windows example; use '/dev/ttyUSB0' on Linux BAUD_RATE = 9600 ser = serial.Serial(SERIAL_PORT, BAUD_RATE, timeout=1) # STEP 2: Maintain a rolling buffer of the last 100 readings MAX_POINTS = 100 timestamps = deque(maxlen=MAX_POINTS) temperatures = deque(maxlen=MAX_POINTS) # STEP 3: Set up the live plot fig, ax = plt.subplots() line, = ax.plot([], [], 'r-') ax.set_xlabel("Sample #") ax.set_ylabel("Temperature (°C)") ax.set_title("KTU PCCPL508 — Live LM35 Data") ax.grid(True) # STEP 4: Regex to parse "T: 27.43 C" pattern = re.compile(r"T:\s*([-+]?\d*\.\d+|\d+)") def update(frame): try: raw = ser.readline().decode('utf-8', errors='ignore').strip() match = pattern.search(raw) if match: t = float(match.group(1)) temperatures.append(t) timestamps.append(len(timestamps)) line.set_data(list(timestamps), list(temperatures)) ax.relim(); ax.autoscale_view() except Exception as e: print("Error:", e) return line, ani = FuncAnimation(fig, update, interval=500) plt.show() ser.close() ``` ### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix — Code-to-Concept Mapping | Code Line | Concept Demonstrated | KTU CO Mapping | |---|---|---| | `Serial.begin(9600)` | UART initialization & baud rate | CO1 — Embedded programming | | `analogRead(LM35_PIN)` | ADC peripheral access | CO2 — Sensor interfacing | | `voltage = adc * 5.0 / 1023` | ADC-to-physical unit conversion | CO3 — Data interpretation | | `Serial.println()` | HMI output to PC | CO4 — Display techniques | | `ser.readline()` | Host-side buffering | CO5 — PC-side integration | | `FuncAnimation` | Real-time data visualization | CO5 — Visualization tools | > [!NOTE] > **Mathematical Sanity Check:** At 25 °C, LM35 outputs 250 mV. ADC code = $\lfloor 0.25/5.0 \times 1023 \rfloor = 51$. Code returns $T = 51/2.046 = 24.93\,°\text{C}$ — within ±0.1 °C of true value. The 0.07 °C offset is due to integer truncation in `analogRead`. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Sensor-to-PC Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
