What happens if the Serial Monitor is set to a different baud rate, say 115200?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** `Serial.begin(9600)` initializes the **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** peripheral of the Arduino with a baud rate of **9600 bits per second**, using the **8N1** frame format (8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit). It configures the TX/RX pins and the ATmega16U2 USB bridge chip so that subsequent `Serial.print()` / `Serial.println()` calls transmit ASCII data to the host PC. **(1 mark)** The baud rate must **match exactly** between the firmware and the Serial Monitor because UART is **asynchronous** — the receiver has no shared clock and instead samples each bit at the center of the bit-time, calculated from the agreed baud rate. **(1 mark)** If the Serial Monitor is set to **115200 bps** while the firmware uses 9600 bps, the receiver's sampling instants are misaligned with the incoming bits. The result is **framing errors**, and the terminal displays **mojibake** (garbled characters such as `ÿÞªÆ¥` or `???`). **(1 mark)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]** **List any three methods to display Arduino/ESP32 sensor data on a PC, and state one advantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Arduino Serial Monitor (IDE)** — Built into the Arduino IDE; *advantage*: zero installation, immediate text output for debugging. **(1 mark)** 2. **Python with `pyserial` + Matplotlib** — A scripted pipeline; *advantage*: enables real-time plotting, CSV logging, and integration with ML pipelines. **(1 mark)** 3. **Processing IDE (Java-based GUI)** — Custom graphical dashboards; *advantage*: full creative control over gauges, dials, and 2D/3D visualizations. **(1 mark)** *(Acceptable alternatives: Node-RED, ThingSpeak cloud, Blynk IoT app, ESP32 Web Server, MATLAB `serial()` object.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete pipeline** for reading a sensor value and displaying it on a PC. Identify the role of the **USB-UART bridge chip**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an **Arduino sketch** to read a **DHT11 temperature & humidity sensor** on pin 2 and display the readings in CSV format on the Serial Monitor every 2 seconds. Include the necessary library include statements. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Block Diagram (descriptive, 3 marks):** $$ \boxed{\text{Sensor} \to \text{MCU ADC} \to \text{UART Frame} \to \text{USB Bridge} \to \text{PC COM Port} \to \text{Terminal App}} $$ - **Sensor (DHT11/LM35):** Converts physical quantity → analog voltage or digital protocol. **(0.5 mark)** - **MCU ADC (ATmega328P):** Samples the analog signal at ~15 kSps with 10-bit resolution, producing integer codes 0–1023. **(1 mark)** - **UART Frame:** Firmware converts ADC code to ASCII string, framed as 8N1 packets. **(0.5 mark)** - **USB-UART Bridge (ATmega16U2 / CH340 / FTDI FT232):** Translates the **3.3 V/5 V TTL UART** lines (RX/TX) into the **USB CDC** protocol so the PC recognizes the device as a virtual COM port. **(1.5 marks)** - **PC Terminal App:** Receives the byte stream and renders the ASCII characters; users see the temperature/humidity values. **(0.5 mark)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Required Library:** `DHT.h` by Adafruit. Install via Library Manager. ```cpp // ============================================================= // KTU PCCPL508 | DHT11 CSV Telemetry // ============================================================= #include "DHT.h" // STEP 1: Include DHT sensor library // STEP 2: Define sensor type and pin #define DHTPIN 2 #define DHTTYPE DHT11 // STEP 3: Instantiate the DHT object DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE); void setup() { // STEP 4: Initialize serial at 9600 bps Serial.begin(9600); // STEP 5: Print CSV header Serial.println("Timestamp_ms,Temperature_C,Humidity_pct"); // STEP 6: Begin DHT sensor dht.begin(); } void loop() { // STEP 7: Wait 2 seconds between samples (DHT11 min interval = 1 s) delay(2000); // STEP 8: Read humidity and temperature float h = dht.readHumidity(); float t = dht.readTemperature(); // °C // STEP 9: Check if any reads failed if (isnan(h) || isnan(t)) { Serial.println("Sensor read failed!"); return; } // STEP 10: Transmit CSV row Serial.print(millis()); // timestamp in ms Serial.print(","); Serial.print(t, 2); // temperature Serial.print(","); Serial.println(h, 2); // humidity } ``` **Mark Split (Part b):** - Library include and object creation: **1 mark** - `Serial.begin(9600)` + CSV header: **1 mark** - `dht.readTemperature()` / `dht.readHumidity()`: **2 marks** - `isnan()` error check: **1 mark** - `Serial.print` CSV formatting + `millis()`: **2 marks** --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **difference between Serial Monitor and Serial Plotter** in Arduino IDE. How is the data formatting different for each? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design and write the **PC-side Python code** using `pyserial` and `matplotlib` to read LM35 temperature data from `COM5` at 9600 baud and plot a **real-time line graph** that auto-updates every 500 ms. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) | Feature | Serial Monitor | Serial Plotter | |---|---|---| | **Output Type** | Plain text (ASCII) | Real-time line graph | | **Data Format** | Free-form text, CSV, JSON | **Comma-separated numeric values** per line (each value = one trace) | | **Use Case** | Debug logs, status messages, strings | Continuous numeric sensor streams | | **X-axis** | None (text scrolls) | Sample index (auto) | | **Multiple Traces** | Not natively supported | Yes — each comma-separated column = a separate colored line | | **Y-axis** | N/A | Auto-scaled | **Key Formatting Rule for Serial Plotter:** Every `Serial.println()` line should contain only **numeric values separated by commas**, e.g., `25.43,60.12`. Text labels are ignored. **(2 marks)** **Example Arduino code for Serial Plotter:** ```cpp void loop() { float t = dht.readTemperature(); float h = dht.readHumidity(); Serial.print(t); // first value Serial.print(","); Serial.println(h); // second value — will appear as a second trace delay(500); } ``` #### Model Solution — Part (b) ```python # ============================================================= # KTU PCCPL508 | Real-Time LM35 Plotter # Library: pip install pyserial matplotlib # ============================================================= import serial import re import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from collections import deque from matplotlib.animation import FuncAnimation # -------- STEP 1: Configure the serial port -------- SERIAL_PORT = "COM5" BAUD_RATE = 9600 ser = serial.Serial(SERIAL_PORT, BAUD_RATE, timeout=1) # -------- STEP 2: Maintain a rolling buffer (last 60 samples) -------- BUFFER_SIZE = 60 temp_buffer = deque(maxlen=BUFFER_SIZE) # -------- STEP 3: Regex to extract temperature from "T: 27.43 C" -------- pattern = re.compile(r"T:\s*([-+]?\d*\.\d+|\d+)") # -------- STEP 4: Initialize the plot -------- fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4)) line, = ax.plot([], [], 'b-', linewidth=2) ax.set_xlabel("Sample Index") ax.set_ylabel("Temperature (°C)") ax.set_title("KTU PCCPL508 — Live LM35 Telemetry") ax.set_ylim(0, 50) ax.grid(True) # -------- STEP 5: Animation callback -------- def update(frame): try: raw = ser.readline().decode('utf-8', errors='ignore').strip() m = pattern.search(raw) if m: temp_buffer.append(float(m.group(1))) line.set_data(range(len(temp_buffer)), list(temp_buffer)) except Exception as e: print("Read error:", e) return line, # -------- STEP 6: Start animation -------- ani = FuncAnimation(fig, update, interval=500) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ser.close() ``` **Mark Split (Part b):** - Correct `serial.Serial()` setup: **1.5 marks** - `deque` rolling buffer: **1 mark** - Regex parsing: **1.5 marks** - `FuncAnimation` with `update()`: **2 marks** - Plot configuration & y-limits: **1 mark** --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting `Serial.begin(9600)` in `setup()`** — Without this, `Serial.print()` outputs nothing and the Serial Monitor stays blank. **[-2 marks]** > 2. **Mismatch between `Serial.begin(x)` and Serial Monitor's dropdown** — Always verify the baud rate dropdown matches the code. Garbage characters = full zero for output. **[-1 mark]** > 3. **Using `float temperature = analogRead(A0) * 5.0 / 1024.0;`** — This is **wrong** because the ADC max code is **1023** (not 1024). Use `1023.0` or `1024.0` consistently with proper offset explanation. **[-1 mark]** > 4. **Forgetting to call `dht.begin()` in `setup()`** — Sensor returns `NaN` (Not a Number). **[-1 mark]** > 5. **Missing `isnan()` check** — If sensor fails, raw `NaN` gets printed and Python's `float()` crashes. **[-1 mark]** > 6. **Wrong COM port in PySerial** — Always instruct students to verify via Arduino IDE → Tools → Port. **[-0.5 mark]** > 7. **Using `Serial.write()` instead of `Serial.print()`** — `write()` sends raw bytes; students see nothing readable. **[-1 mark]** --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** > > ✅ **Core Pipeline:** Sensor → ADC → ASCII → UART → USB Bridge → COM Port → Terminal App > > ✅ **Baud Rate Rule:** Firmware baud **MUST** match Serial Monitor baud — default **9600 8N1** > > ✅ **ADC Formula:** $V = \text{ADC} \times 5.0 / 1023$ (Arduino Uno, $V_{\text{ref}} = 5\,\text{V}$, 10-bit) > > ✅ **LM35 Equation:** $T(°\text{C}) = V_{\text{out}} / 0.01$ (10 mV/°C linear scale) > > ✅ **DHT11:** Digital single-wire protocol, 1 Hz max sampling, library `DHT.h` required > > ✅ **Serial.print vs Serial.println:** `print` = no newline, `println` = appends `\r\n` > > ✅ **Serial Plotter Format:** Comma-separated numerics ONLY — no text labels in data lines > > ✅ **PySerial Boilerplate:** `ser = serial.Serial('COM5', 9600, timeout=1)` + `ser.readline().decode('utf-8')` > > ✅ **Error Handling:** Always check `isnan()` for DHT; always use `try/except` in Python > > ✅ **CSV Format:** `timestamp, sensor1, sensor2, ...` — ideal for Excel/Pandas post-processing > > ✅ **USB-UART Bridge:** ATmega16U2 (Uno R3), CH340G (clones), FTDI FT232 (older boards) > > ✅ **Alternative PC Tools:** Processing (Java GUI), Node-RED (flow-based), MATLAB `serial()` object, Blynk (mobile), ThingSpeak (cloud) > > ✅ **ESP32 Bonus:** Can host a **Wi-Fi web server**, eliminating the USB cable entirely — sensor data accessible from any browser on the LAN at `http:///` ---
